critique.summary: The article describes how the author built a custom AI receptionist named Axle for their brother's luxury mechanic shop to answer calls and reduce lost business. The AI uses a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline with a knowledge base of the shop's services and pricing, integrated with telephony and voice platforms to provide accurate, conversational responses and handle callbacks.
mainPoints: The mechanic shop owner was losing thousands monthly due to missed calls while busy working.
The AI receptionist, Axle, was custom-built using a RAG pipeline to ensure accurate answers based on a structured knowledge base scraped from the shop's website.
The knowledge base was embedded into MongoDB Atlas with vector search to retrieve relevant documents for customer queries.
Anthropic Claude was used to generate responses strictly grounded in the knowledge base to avoid hallucinations.
The AI was connected to a real phone number using the Vapi platform, handling speech-to-text and text-to-speech.
Conversation memory and callback logging were implemented to manage follow-ups and improve customer service.
Voice tuning was done to select an appropriate AI voice and rewrite prompts for natural spoken responses.
Integration tests were created to ensure robustness and handle edge cases.
Future plans include adding calendar booking, SMS notifications, a dashboard, security improvements, and deployment for production use.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a detailed, technically plausible account of building a custom AI receptionist using current AI and telephony technologies. The described tools and methods, such as RAG pipelines, vector search in MongoDB Atlas, use of Anthropic Claude for response generation, and integration with Vapi for telephony, are consistent with known capabilities of these technologies as of 2024. The claims about lost business due to missed calls and the benefits of an AI receptionist are reasonable but anecdotal and specific to the author's brother's business. There are no extraordinary or unverifiable claims that would undermine the article's credibility.
considerations: The article does not provide independent verification or data on the actual impact of the AI receptionist on call handling or revenue recovery.
Potential privacy and security implications of recording and storing call data are not discussed in detail.
The scalability and reliability of the system in a real production environment beyond the development phase are not addressed.
User experience feedback from actual customers interacting with the AI receptionist is not included.
The article does not mention any limitations or challenges encountered beyond voice tuning.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the practicality and challenges of implementing an AI receptionist for a mechanic shop. Commenters debate the accuracy and usefulness of such systems, the risks involved, and alternative approaches like voicemail systems or human receptionists. Some praise the innovation and potential cost savings, while others caution about limitations and customer trust issues.

comments.author: throwaboat
comments.text:
I used to work as a service advisor - or as the article says, receptionist. This system will not work as described for several reasons.

1. Unless you have a recent job that matches the exact same repair/service, you have incorrectly estimated the cost of the repair. In some states, this matters a lot and will cost the shop money. Unless your LLM only quotes for labor in sane amounts for diagnostic and nothing else, you’re only adding noise. This is a disservice to the client and the shop owner. The client now has an inaccurate quote for work and the shop will get a reputation for being inaccurate in quoting work.

2. Let’s say that you manage to get the exact same job twice. Your machine now needs to source parts. Parts may have been in stock yesterday. The might be out of stock now. If they are in stock, you need to retotal the price since prices are dynamic. Did you teach the agent how to source parts? What rules does it have for sourcing used parts?

3. New jobs can’t be quoted. Even if you taught the machine how to calculate book time and margins, it still has to find the right parts. If your shop does high end work, you know how much of a pain in the ass this is. Also remember that some work requires nonobvious parts - like fluids if you need to remove a part in the way of your goal.

4. The only area I see this being useful in is pickup. The shop can mark a car as done and the LLM can call to inform the client that they can come at a preset, unchanging time to get the vehicle. If the vehicle is staying overnight, the LLM can call with a progress update.

Finally, I’d like to note that this sort of dev work goes beyond hubris. It’s dangerous. The more we assume we know without verifying, the greater the risk. In this case, the dev is risking someone else’s livelihood.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, experience-based critique highlighting practical limitations and risks, making it highly accurate and constructive.

comments.author: thestack_ai
comments.text: he hard part of voice AI agents is not building the first demo. It is handling the cases your training data did not cover. We run multi-agent systems daily and the pattern is always the same. Happy path works great on day one, then edge cases pile up for months. Having a clear escalation path to a human like you did is the right call
comments.analysis: Accurately identifies a common challenge in AI voice agents and supports the use of human escalation, reflecting practical deployment experience.

comments.author: singpolyma3
comments.text:
They admit a raw LLM would be dangerous and then proceed to use RAG... How is this any better? You cannot allow an LLM to generate the final outbound message if you are liable for what it says.

LLM to understand the question? Yes. Generate SQL maybe with Embeddings to look up answers? Yes. Generate the final response? No.
comments.analysis: Raises important ethical and liability concerns about AI-generated responses, emphasizing cautious system design.

comments.author: mamonster
comments.text:
>and he’s losing thousands of dollars per month because he misses hundreds of calls per week. He’s under the hood all day. The phone rings, he can’t answer, the customer hangs up and calls someone else. That’s a lost job — sometimes a $450 brake service, sometimes a $2,000 engine repair — just gone because no one picked up.

How much does it cost to have an outsourced receptionist? Even if it is 500 a month if we are really talking about thousands of dollars per month lost your ROI is still crazy.
comments.analysis: Provides a practical cost-benefit perspective comparing AI solutions to human receptionists.

comments.author: christoff12
comments.text:
Reading this thread has me wondering if a smarter voicemail inbox system would be a viable and useful alternative.

Something like leave a voicemail and get a ticket created in the system to log the call for triage once the owner's hands are free.

The system could be configured to text a link to an intake form or quote calculator or calendly or whatever if intent is successfully detected.

I wouldn't mind this as a caller because I'd hear a typical phone tree setup, have a callback option, __and__ have a digital action I can take without being annoyed by a fake receptionist.

I imagine a certain type of business owner would appreciate the more streamlined workflow, plus having more control without the AI risks.
comments.analysis: Suggests a pragmatic alternative that balances automation with user control and trust.

comments.author: cdrnsf
comments.text: Our local air conditioning/heating/plumbing place started routing their frontline answering service to an AI call service. It has an odd uncanny valley feeling to it that I simply don't trust. I chased down other points of contact until I got ahold of a person instead. I'd trust a phone tree with a voicemail box more.
comments.analysis: Reflects common user discomfort with AI voice agents, highlighting trust as a key factor.

comments.author: allanrbo
comments.text: I wish all shops just have a clear email address. Id much prefer emailing over placing a voice call...
comments.analysis: Offers a user preference that suggests alternative communication channels may be more effective.

comments.author: jorisboris
comments.text:
At the moment I'm pretty inclined to hang up if I feel I'm wasting my time with a robot.

But maybe soon we will not even realise we speak to a robot, given the current speed of ai development.

I wonder how that will erode trust in calls. I moved from cold emailing and cold LinkedIn to cold calling because of the massive amounts of ai spam I have to compete with. But maybe cold calling will die soon as well if the robots emerge.
comments.analysis: Insightful speculation on the social impact of AI voice agents on trust and communication norms.

comments.author: faronel
comments.text:
The amount of negative comments here to someone building something is incredible.

I appreciated your post and have some takeaways around text formatting for TTS in my own projects. Thanks!
comments.analysis: Offers a positive and supportive perspective, encouraging innovation despite criticism.

comments.author: gverrilla
comments.text:
A lot of incel attacks on this post, only because it's a woman. The same mf's who would be licking the balls of a guy who built a USD30k screen to show him the weather...

It was a nice read, thatlady.dev, and I'm happy for your experience - thank you!
comments.analysis: Focuses on perceived gender bias rather than technical content, adding little substantive analysis.
